title: The Macro Minute: Is the global liquidity cycle rolling over?

Happy Friday out there, team 42. It's
your skipper here at Darius Dell to
present our macro minute for Friday,
November 14th, 2025. Hope everyone's
having a great week. So, as always,
we'll start with the executive summary
from today's leadup morning note. Uh, so
let's hop right in. Today's key macro
question is, is the global liquidity
cycle rolling over? The short answer is
it certainly is at the margin. Whether
the nent deterioration devolves into a
durable liquidity cycle downturn is up
to the Fed and to a lesser extent the
PBOC. Terms of the analytical nuance,
the selloff in risk assets accelerated
into Friday as investors increasingly
pricing in a directionally hawkish pivot
by the Federal Reserve drove the S&P 500
toward its first consecutive weekly
decline since June when we observed the
first major wave of the upside
capitulation we called for in early May.
Amid our then two week old gold paradigm
C theme, traders cut implied odds for a
December US rate cut to sub 50% after
multiple Federal Reserve officials
questioned the justification for a third
consecutive move by citing ongoing
economic durability and sticky inflation
dynamics. Morning markets, which had
nearly fully discounted a 25 basis point
cut less than one month ago, now shift
their focus uh to Friday remarks from
three Federal Reserve officials,
including voting member Jeffrey Schmid,
who desented against last month's 25
basis point cut. Investors also await
await a revised timeable for economic
data releases postponed by the US
federal government shutdown. Elsewhere,
China's economy lost more momentum than
projected at the start of the fourth
quarter as an unprecedented slump in
investment combined with softer
manufacturing and weaker consumer
spending deepen drag deepen the drag on
overall activity. Uh the economy is
facing quite a few challenges given the
multiple unstable [laughter] unstable I
think they know we're talking about here
and uncertain factors in the external
environment and rather big pressures on
economic restructuring within the
country. China's National Bureau of
Statistics said in a statement
accompanying the data releases. It added
that officials would actively facilitate
the implementation of existing policies
signaling Beijing is not yet prepared to
offer additional support. Recall that
Chinese authorities attempted to counter
the investment downturn by approving 1
trillion yuan of stimulus in late
September. The likelihood of further
monetary easing has faded after the PBOC
indicated it was not troubled by weaker
trends in loan growth observed in the
October credit data. China is clearly
pulling back from a liquidity
perspective now that Beijing's roughly
5% growth target for 2025 appears
achievable, especially following the
October trade troops with the US. As
always, members of our global investor
community can trust that our
institutional grade risk management
overlays, kiss and Dr. Mo, will help our
portfolios navigate these emergent risks
better than investors attempting to
manage risk without systematic rules or
relying on fundamental research alone.
Transitioning to our 42 micro dashboard

our community. Uh this was not really a
question, more of a comment, but I
thought it was uh important to share,
especially given the uh pickup in
volatility we've observed and and

recent weeks. Uh so um this one just

complements the quant qualitative
analysis. Being on the right side of
markets consistently builds wealth over
time. Amen. No one knows when the next
draw down is the 50% bare market. But
you have to be willing to accept that
markets move quickly and tomorrow new
information may change the investment
thesis and changes to the portfolio.
You'd rather have a false alarm than a
massive draw down. This is what we talk
about when we talk about type one errors
versus type two errors. Cutting losses
quickly and letting winners run is
always the right way to handle trends.
Capturing 80% of the upside and only 20%
of the downside. Uh simplification of

three core things that matter to you as

maybe you don't realize it yet, but you
know, it's your spiritual health, it's

financial health. I would rank them in

is contributing to uh the spiritual
health of the folks in our global
investment community. But obviously the

of the positive impact we're having on
the financial health of many thousands
of investors around the world. Uh we're
so I'm so incredibly blessed by that. Um
this is why we build a company uh is to
create these kinds of positive outcomes
uh for uh ordinary investors. You know I
spent the first 12 years of my career
effectively making rich people richer
and it was great. I made a lot of money
doing that too. Uh but you know there
was a big emptiness inside considering
my background and and how I was uh
raised and you know you know basically
at the very bottom of the uh
increasingly K-shaped US economy. And so
as someone who's made it to the top of
the K, part of the K, you know, it
doesn't sit well with me, uh, that, you
know, I spend all day, 12, 13, 14,
sometimes 15, 16 hours a day working
only to make rich people rich. And so,
um, you know, about half a decade ago, I
left u to start this company and and and
and the rest is history in terms of the
incredibly positive impact, uh, that

and spiritual well-being of of thousands
of investors around the world and our

guys for tuning in to these macro
